task.title: Migrating Content from Unstructured XML to AI-Ready DITA: Step by Step
task.shortdesc: A complete, repeatable process for converting unstructured XML files into semantically rich, AI-ready DITA topics with full prolog metadata.

prereq.p: Before starting migration, ensure you have:

ul.li: DITA-OT 4.x installed and working (dita --version)
A taxonomy/subject scheme defining your controlled vocabulary (optional — if you do not have one yet, use the starter taxonomy below)
Your complete set of unstructured XML or HTML source files
A clear understanding of your target audience types

prereq.note:
No taxonomy yet? You can start with this minimal subject scheme and refine it as you migrate more content. Save this as subjectScheme.ditamap in your project: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> <!DOCTYPE subjectScheme PUBLIC "-//OASIS//DTD DITA Subject Scheme Map//EN" "subjectScheme.dtd"> <subjectScheme> <subjectdef keys="difficulty"> <subjectdef keys="beginner"/> <subjectdef keys="intermediate"/> <subjectdef keys="advanced"/> </subjectdef> <subjectdef keys="content-intent"> <subjectdef keys="explain"/> <subjectdef keys="how-to"/> <subjectdef keys="reference"/> <subjectdef keys="troubleshoot"/> </subjectdef> <subjectdef keys="audience-type"> <subjectdef keys="technical-writer"/> <subjectdef keys="developer"/> <subjectdef keys="manager"/> </subjectdef> </subjectScheme>
A subject scheme is simply a DITA map that defines allowed values — like a picklist. It ensures every author uses the same vocabulary for difficulty, intent, and audience metadata. You will learn more about subject schemes in Taxonomy and Controlled Vocabulary.

context.p: This migration process was developed and refined while converting the Extense training content from 20+ unstructured XML files to 76 AI-ready DITA topics. Each step produces a measurable improvement in AI readiness. The steps are ordered from foundational (structure) to advanced (AI optimization) so that each step builds on the previous one.

step.cmd: Audit your source content and classify each file by information type.

info.p: Read every source XML file and determine whether its primary purpose is to:

ul.li: Explain a concept → will become a <concept>
Teach a procedure → will become a <task>
Provide lookup/reference data → will become a <reference>

info.p: Create a spreadsheet mapping each source file to its DITA type:
info.codeblock: dita_intro_guide.xml → concept (dita-intro-guide.dita) installing_dita_ot.xml → task (installing-dita-ot.dita) dita-ot-parameters.xml → reference (dita-ot-parameters.dita) dita_reuse_variants.xml → concept (dita-reuse-variants.dita)

step.stepresult: You have a complete mapping of source files to target DITA types.

step.cmd: Convert the structural markup from source XML to DITA XML.

info.p: For each file, perform these structural conversions:

step.stepresult: Valid DITA XML files that pass structural validation (dita --input file.dita -f html5).

info.p: The shortdesc is critical for AI — it is prepended to every chunk from that topic as a summary. A good shortdesc:

ul.li: Is one clear sentence (25-35 words)
Describes the topic's purpose, not just its title
Contains key search terms
Works as a standalone summary when shown in search results

info.p: Before (no shortdesc): Chunks from this topic have no summary context.
info.codeblock: <shortdesc>How to write content once and assemble it into multiple deliverables using conref, keyref, and DITAVAL filtering.</shortdesc>

step.stepresult: Every topic has a shortdesc that functions as a chunk summary.

step.cmd: Add xml:lang="en-US" to every root element.

info.p: This attribute enables NLP language detection in the RAG pipeline. Without it, the embedding model cannot confirm the content language.

step.cmd: Add the full AI-ready prolog metadata to every topic.

info.p: This is the most impactful step. Each topic needs this complete <prolog> block:
info.codeblock: <prolog> <author>Extense LLC</author> <critdates> <created date="2026-02-10"/> </critdates> <metadata> <keywords> <indexterm>Term 1</indexterm> <indexterm>Term 2</indexterm> <indexterm>Term 3</indexterm> </keywords> <category>taxonomy:content-domain</category> <audience>target-audience</audience> </metadata> <resourceid appid="extense-training" appname="chatbot" id="topic-id"/> <data name="difficulty" value="intermediate"/> <data name="duration" value="PT20M"/> <data name="content-domain" value="publishing"/> <data name="content-intent" value="explain"/> <data name="chatbot-priority" value="high"/> <data name="service-line" value="publishing-engineering"/> <data name="prerequisites" value="previous-topic-id"/> </prolog>
info.p: Choose metadata values from the controlled vocabulary defined in the subject scheme taxonomy. Each field has a specific purpose for AI:

ul.li: difficulty: beginner | intermediate | advanced | expert — enables level-appropriate retrieval
duration: ISO 8601 format (PT15M = 15 minutes) — helps users plan learning time
content-domain: subject area for domain-filtered retrieval
content-intent: explain | how-to | reference | troubleshoot | best-practice | tutorial — enables intent routing
chatbot-priority: high | medium | low — weights retrieval ranking
service-line: links content to business service offerings
prerequisites: topic-id of prerequisite content — enables learning path sequencing

step.stepresult: Every topic has complete, taxonomy-aligned metadata that the RAG pipeline can filter on.

step.cmd: Ensure every <section> has both an @id and a <title>.

info.p: The chunking pipeline splits content at section boundaries. Each section becomes a separately retrievable chunk. Sections without IDs cannot be deep-linked. Sections without titles produce chunks that lack context labels.
info.codeblock:
Conref allows you to...

How does the conref mechanism work?

Conref allows you to...

step.stepresult: Every section is addressable, titled, and produces a well-labeled chunk.

info.p: This enables syntax highlighting in HTML output and language detection in RAG chunks:
info.codeblock: <codeblock outputclass="language-bash">dita --input map.ditamap -f html5</codeblock> <codeblock outputclass="language-xml"><concept id="..."></codeblock> <codeblock outputclass="language-xslt"><xsl:template match="..."></codeblock>

step.cmd: Build the DITA map that defines topic organization and learning paths.

info.p: Create a bookmap or map that organizes topics into a progressive learning sequence:
The map hierarchy tells the chatbot about content relationships — which topics belong together and in what order they should be learned.

step.stepresult: A complete DITA map defining the information architecture and learning path.

step.cmd: Validate by building HTML5 output and testing the chatbot ingestion pipeline.

ol.li: Build HTML5 to verify structural validity: dita -i map.ditamap -f html5 -o output/
Run the chatbot ingestion script to verify metadata extraction and chunking: python3 dita-to-chatbot-json.py --input dita-topics --output chatbot-data

info.p: Inspect the ingestion-report.json for chunks missing metadata, short chunks, or other quality issues.

step.stepresult: All topics build without errors and produce metadata-rich chunks in the chatbot pipeline.

result.p: After completing these steps, your content has been transformed from unstructured XML into AI-ready DITA topics. Every topic is:

ul.li: Semantically typed (concept, task, or reference)
Metadata-enriched with audience, difficulty, domain, intent, and priority
Section-structured for optimal RAG chunking
Organized into maps for learning path sequencing
Validated for both HTML output and chatbot ingestion

postreq.p: Once migration is complete, establish ongoing governance:

ul.li: Create a topic template with the full prolog metadata as a starting point for new topics
Add metadata validation to your CI/CD pipeline to prevent metadata-lean topics from being published
Schedule quarterly AI-readiness audits using the scoring framework from the maturity model
